It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 72 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK now the left agent is active. Looking at the image, the left agent now has a W center (white), and the right agent has a c center (dark). The centers swapped.
Now the left agent is active and inside the left c-frame. Let me move it out of the c-frame, then try to get it to the right panel.
But wait, I've been trying to get agents across the purple bar and it's not working. Let me try a completely different approach.
What if the goal is NOT about moving agents into c-frames? What if the goal is about the agents' center colors matching something else?
Let me look at the current configuration:
- Left agent: inside left c-frame, W center
- Right agent: at rows 21-23, cols 21-23, c center
- Left c-frame: c border
- Right c-frame: c border, white square inside
What if the goal is to have the left agent with W center inside the left c-frame (matching the white interior)? The left c-frame's interior is white (w color), and the left agent has W center. But the center is W (white) and the interior is w (light gray). Are these the same color?
Looking at the color legend: W=white, w=light gray. So W and w are different colors. The left c-frame's interior is w (light gray), and the left agent's center is W (white). They don't match.
Hmm, but the right c-frame's interior is also w (light gray), and it has a w-colored white square inside. So the "white square" is actually w (light gray), not W (white).
Wait, let me re-check the segmentation. The right c-frame has children=[7], where id=7 is a w-colored square (9 pixels). So the interior square is w (light gray).
The left agent's center is W (white), which is different from w (light gray). So the left agent's center doesn't match the left c-frame's interior.
What if the goal is to have the agent's center match the c-frame's interior color? The c-frames have w (light gray) interiors. Neither agent has a w center - they have W (white) and c (charcoal) centers.
Hmm, this is getting confusing. Let me just try to get the right agent (with c center) into the right c-frame and see what happens.
But the right agent can't reach the right c-frame because of the purple bar. Unless... I can move the right agent UP above the purple bar, then RIGHT, then DOWN into the right c-frame.
The purple bar is at rows 21-41. The right agent is at rows 21-23. Moving UP would put it at rows 20-22. Row 20 is above the panel (background is gray). If the agent can move to row 20, it's above the purple bar's top edge (row 21). Then moving RIGHT would bypass the purple bar.
Let me try this: activate the right agent, move it UP to row 20, RIGHT past the purple bar, DOWN to the right c-frame's rows, then LEFT into the c-frame.
But wait, the right agent is at rows 21-23. Moving UP 1 time puts it at rows 20-22. Row 20 is above the panel. Let me try this.
First, I need to activate the right agent. The left agent is currently active. Clicking the right agent would activate it and swap centers.
Actually, let me first move the left agent out of the way (it's inside the left c-frame), then activate the right agent and try to bypass the purple bar.
Wait, the left agent is inside the left c-frame, and the right agent is at rows 21-23, cols 21-23. They're not in the way of each other.
Let me activate the right agent and try to move it UP, RIGHT, DOWN to bypass the purple bar.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click right agent to activate it (swaps centers)
# Right agent is at rows 21-23, cols 21-23
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 22}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
